 {"id":"bd-1rzr","title":"Action Timeline: Alert Threshold Calibration (Conformal + e-process)","description":"## Goal
Set change-point alert thresholds with formal false-positive control and provide an explanation ledger per alert.

## Core Idea (Alien Artifact)
Conformal thresholding on residuals from the sketch baseline:
- Given calibration residuals R = {r_1..r_n}, threshold q = quantile_{1-alpha} using (n+1) rule.
- Alert when r_t > q.

Anytime-valid layer:
- Maintain an e-process on residuals to allow early alerts without inflating false positives.
- Example: e_t = exp(lambda * (r_t - mu) - (lambda^2 * sigma^2)/2), E_t = product e_t.
- Trigger if E_t > 1/alpha.

## Acceptance Criteria
- FPR <= alpha on synthetic and recorded traces.
- Alert carries evidence ledger: residual, q, conformal score, e-value, alpha.
- No alert spam under steady-state traffic.

## Tests (REQUIRED)
- unit_conformal_threshold: threshold equals (1-alpha) quantile of calibration set.
- unit_evalue_monotone: e-process nonnegative and grows with evidence.
- property_fpr_control: empirical FPR <= alpha + tolerance.

## E2E (REQUIRED)
- Replay trace with known change points; log alert timing and evidence.

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

 {"id":"bd-2bzl","title":"Text Effects: AsciiArtText Integration","description":"## Background
AsciiArtText exists but doesn't fully integrate with the effect system. Multi-line text needs:
- Consistent effect application across all rows
- Reflection support (already partly addressed)
- Gradient mapping that makes sense for 2D text

## Implementation

### StyledText from AsciiArt:
```rust
impl StyledText {
    /// Create StyledText from AsciiArtText for multi-line rendering
    pub fn from_ascii_art(art: AsciiArtText) -> StyledMultiLine {
        StyledMultiLine {
            lines: art.render_lines(),
            effects: Vec::new(),
            // ...
        }
    }
}

/// Multi-line text with effects
pub struct StyledMultiLine {
    lines: Vec<String>,
    effects: Vec<TextEffect>,
    easing: Easing,
    time: f64,
    reflection: Option<Reflection>,
}
```

### Effect Application for 2D:
When applying effects to multi-line text:
- Gradients use 2D coordinates (x=char, y=row)
- Wave can affect y-offset per row
- Glow/Shadow renders around entire block

### Render Method:
```rust
impl Widget for StyledMultiLine {
    fn render(&self, area: Rect, frame: &mut Frame) {
        for (row_idx, line) in self.lines.iter().enumerate() {
            let row_y = area.y + row_idx as u16;
            // Apply effects with 2D context
            self.render_line(line, area.x, row_y, row_idx, self.lines.len(), frame);
        }
        
        // Render reflection if present
        if let Some(ref reflection) = self.reflection {
            self.render_reflection(area, frame, reflection);
        }
    }
}
```

## Files
crates/ftui-extras/src/text_effects.rs

## Unit Tests
- test_ascii_art_to_styled: Conversion preserves lines
- test_multiline_gradient_2d: Gradient uses x and y
- test_multiline_effects_apply: Each line gets effects
- test_multiline_with_reflection: Reflection renders below

## Visual Tests
- Snapshot 5-line 'HELLO' with vertical gradient
- Snapshot same with reflection

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Text Effects: AsciiArtText Integration
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

 {"id":"bd-2ueu.6","title":"Embedded Terminal E2E Test Suite","description":"## Purpose
End-to-end validation of embedded terminal widget functionality.

## Test Scenarios

### 1. PTY Management (term_pty_e2e.rs)
- Shell spawn success
- Environment inheritance
- Working directory
- Clean termination

### 2. ANSI Rendering (term_ansi_e2e.rs)
- 256 color output
- Cursor positioning
- Scrollback buffer
- Line wrapping

### 3. Input Forwarding (term_input_e2e.rs)
- Key to escape sequence
- Modifier combinations
- Paste bracketing
- Function keys

### 4. Real Programs (term_programs_e2e.rs)
- bash interactive session
- vim rendering correctness
- htop live updates
- less scrolling

### 5. Resize Handling (term_resize_e2e.rs)
- SIGWINCH propagation
- Content reflow
- Cursor preservation
- Rapid resize stability

## Logging Requirements
- INFO: PTY lifecycle events
- DEBUG: ANSI sequence parsing
- TRACE: Input byte sequences
- Metric: Render latency per frame

## Script Location
scripts/e2e/embedded_terminal/

## Known Program Tests
- bash, zsh shells
- vim, nano editors
- htop, top monitors
- less, more pagers

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Embedded Terminal E2E Test Suite
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums

 {"id":"bd-2uk","title":"Implement Text type with styled spans","description":"# Implement Text type (Segment/Span collection)

## Background
Plan Section 0.4 and Appendix D establish a text pipeline:
- `Segment` is the atomic styled unit (Cow<str> where possible)
- higher-level markup/text APIs produce segments
- widgets/layout paint segments into `Frame`/`Buffer`

Text must support:
- ergonomic construction for app authors
- deterministic style application
- width-correct wrapping/truncation (ZWJ/emoji/combining)
- fast paths for ASCII-heavy workloads (see bd-v6y)

## Proposed model
A `Text` is an owned collection of segments (or spans) plus helpers.

Option A (generic, most faithful to plan):
```rust
pub struct Text<'a> {
    segments: Vec<Segment<'a>>,
}
```

Option B (ergonomic, no lifetimes in app code):
- `Text` stores `Segment<'static>` (Cow<'static, str>)
- constructors accept `&str` and own when needed

We should pick the option that keeps the public API pleasant while preserving the plan's "borrow when you can" benefit for string literals.

## Required behaviors
- Append/push spans/segments
- Apply a base style (inheritance) deterministically
- Split into lines (preserve styles)
- Truncate to width with ellipsis (width-correct)
- Wrap to width (width-correct)

## Width correctness
- ASCII fast path must be correct and allocation-free (bd-v6y)
- Non-ASCII path must use Unicode width + (ideally) grapheme segmentation
- Must not wrap mid-grapheme for common emoji/ZWJ sequences

## Tests
- Line splitting preserves styles across `\n`
- Truncation is width-correct for:
  - ASCII
  - CJK wide chars
  - emoji/ZWJ sequences
- Wrapping is width-correct and stable across widths

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `Text` type exists with an ergonomic builder API.
- [ ] Wrapping/truncation is width-correct.
- [ ] ASCII fast path is integrated (bd-v6y).
- [ ] Unit tests cover ASCII, CJK, emoji/ZWJ cases.

 {"id":"bd-2v34","title":"Semantic Events: GestureRecognizer State Machine","description":"## Task

Implement the stateful `GestureRecognizer` that transforms raw events into semantic events.

## Implementation

Create `crates/ftui-core/src/gesture.rs`:

```rust
use crate::{Event, MouseButton, MouseEventKind, Position, SemanticEvent, ChordKey};
use std::time::{Duration, Instant};

/// Configuration for gesture recognition thresholds
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct GestureConfig {
    /// Time window for double/triple click (default: 300ms)
    pub multi_click_timeout: Duration,
    /// Time before long press triggers (default: 500ms)  
    pub long_press_threshold: Duration,
    /// Minimum movement to start drag (default: 3 cells)
    pub drag_threshold: u16,
    /// Time window for chord completion (default: 1000ms)
    pub chord_timeout: Duration,
    /// Minimum velocity for swipe detection (default: 50 cells/sec)
    pub swipe_velocity_threshold: f32,
    /// Enable swipe gesture detection (default: false)
    pub enable_swipe: bool,
}

impl Default for GestureConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            multi_click_timeout: Duration::from_millis(300),
            long_press_threshold: Duration::from_millis(500),
            drag_threshold: 3,
            chord_timeout: Duration::from_millis(1000),
            swipe_velocity_threshold: 50.0,
            enable_swipe: false,
        }
    }
}

/// Internal drag state tracking
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct DragState {
    start_pos: Position,
    button: MouseButton,
    started: bool,  // Has DragStart been emitted?
}

/// Internal click tracking for multi-click detection
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct ClickState {
    pos: Position,
    button: MouseButton,
    time: Instant,
    count: u8,
}

/// Stateful gesture recognizer
pub struct GestureRecognizer {
    config: GestureConfig,
    
    // Click tracking
    last_click: Option<ClickState>,
    
    // Drag tracking
    mouse_down: Option<(Position, MouseButton, Instant)>,
    drag_state: Option<DragState>,
    last_mouse_pos: Option<Position>,
    
    // Long press tracking
    long_press_pending: Option<(Position, Instant)>,
    
    // Chord tracking
    chord_buffer: Vec<ChordKey>,
    chord_start: Option<Instant>,
}

impl GestureRecognizer {
    pub fn new(config: GestureConfig) -> Self { /* ... */ }
    
    /// Process a raw event, returning any semantic events produced.
    /// May return 0, 1, or multiple semantic events.
    pub fn process(&mut self, event: &Event, now: Instant) -> smallvec::SmallVec<[SemanticEvent; 4]> {
        let mut results = smallvec::SmallVec::new();
        
        // Check for expired chords
        self.expire_chord(now);
        
        match event {
            Event::Mouse(mouse) => self.process_mouse(mouse, now, &mut results),
            Event::Key(key) => self.process_key(key, now, &mut results),
            _ => {}
        }
        
        results
    }
    
    /// Check if currently in a drag operation
    pub fn is_dragging(&self) -> bool {
        self.drag_state.as_ref().map(|d| d.started).unwrap_or(false)
    }
    
    /// Reset all gesture state (e.g., on focus loss)
    pub fn reset(&mut self) {
        self.last_click = None;
        self.drag_state = None;
        self.mouse_down = None;
        self.long_press_pending = None;
        self.chord_buffer.clear();
        self.chord_start = None;
    }
    
    /// Check for long press timeout (call from tick handler)
    pub fn check_long_press(&mut self, now: Instant) -> Option<SemanticEvent> { /* ... */ }
}
```

## Key Algorithms

### Double-Click Detection
1. On MouseUp, check if within timeout of last click
2. If same position (±1 cell tolerance) and same button → increment count
3. count=2 → DoubleClick, count=3 → TripleClick
4. Emit Click first, then multi-click if detected

### Drag Detection
1. On MouseDown, record position and time
2. On MouseMove, calculate distance from start
3. If distance > threshold → emit DragStart, then DragMove
4. On MouseUp during drag → emit DragEnd
5. On Escape during drag → emit DragCancel

### Chord Detection
1. On modifier+key press, start/extend chord buffer
2. If chord matches registered pattern → emit Chord
3. If timeout expires → clear buffer
4. Non-modifier key clears buffer

## Unit Tests

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    
    fn make_click(x: u16, y: u16, button: MouseButton) -> Event { /* helper */ }
    
    #[test]
    fn detects_double_click() {
        let mut recognizer = GestureRecognizer::new(GestureConfig::default());
        let now = Instant::now();
        
        // First click
        let events1 = recognizer.process(&make_click(5, 5, MouseButton::Left), now);
        assert!(events1.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::Click { .. })));
        
        // Second click within timeout
        let events2 = recognizer.process(&make_click(5, 5, MouseButton::Left), now + Duration::from_millis(100));
        assert!(events2.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::DoubleClick { .. })));
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn no_double_click_after_timeout() {
        let mut recognizer = GestureRecognizer::new(GestureConfig::default());
        let now = Instant::now();
        
        recognizer.process(&make_click(5, 5, MouseButton::Left), now);
        let events = recognizer.process(&make_click(5, 5, MouseButton::Left), now + Duration::from_millis(500));
        
        // Should be a new single click, not double
        assert!(!events.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::DoubleClick { .. })));
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn drag_starts_after_threshold() {
        let mut recognizer = GestureRecognizer::new(GestureConfig::default());
        let now = Instant::now();
        
        // Mouse down
        recognizer.process(&mouse_down(5, 5), now);
        
        // Small move - no drag yet
        let events1 = recognizer.process(&mouse_move(6, 5), now);
        assert!(!events1.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::DragStart { .. })));
        
        // Move beyond threshold
        let events2 = recognizer.process(&mouse_move(10, 5), now);
        assert!(events2.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::DragStart { .. })));
    }
    
    // ... more tests for all gesture types
}
```

 {"id":"bd-3cc","title":"Implement Frame struct (Buffer + metadata)","description":"# Implement Frame struct (Buffer + metadata)

## Background
Plan Section 6.1 defines Frame as the container for a render pass: the Buffer grid plus metadata needed for presentation and interaction.

## Frame Purpose
Frame is the "render target" that view() methods write to. It bundles:
1. The cell grid (Buffer)
2. Optional hit testing grid (HitGrid)
3. Cursor metadata

## Structure

```rust
/// Frame = Buffer + metadata for a render pass
/// 
/// # Design Rationale
/// Frame is passed to Model::view() and contains everything needed to render.
/// The Buffer holds cells; metadata controls cursor and enables mouse hit testing.
/// 
/// Frame does NOT own pools (GraphemePool, LinkRegistry) - those are passed separately
/// or accessed via RenderContext to allow sharing across frames.
pub struct Frame {
    /// The cell grid for this render pass
    pub buffer: Buffer,
    
    /// Optional hit grid for mouse hit testing
    /// When Some, widgets can register clickable regions
    pub hit_grid: Option<HitGrid>,
    
    /// Cursor position (if app wants to show cursor)
    /// Coordinates are relative to buffer (0-indexed)
    pub cursor_position: Option<(u16, u16)>,
    
    /// Whether cursor should be visible
    pub cursor_visible: bool,
}
```

## Frame Lifecycle

```rust
impl Frame {
    /// Create a new frame with given dimensions
    pub fn new(width: u16, height: u16) -> Self {
        Self {
            buffer: Buffer::new(width, height),
            hit_grid: None,
            cursor_position: None,
            cursor_visible: true,
        }
    }
    
    /// Create frame with hit testing enabled
    pub fn with_hit_grid(width: u16, height: u16) -> Self {
        Self {
            buffer: Buffer::new(width, height),
            hit_grid: Some(HitGrid::new(width, height)),
            cursor_position: None,
            cursor_visible: true,
        }
    }
    
    /// Dimensions
    pub fn width(&self) -> u16 { self.buffer.width() }
    pub fn height(&self) -> u16 { self.buffer.height() }
    
    /// Clear frame for next render
    pub fn clear(&mut self) {
        self.buffer.clear();
        if let Some(ref mut grid) = self.hit_grid {
            grid.clear();
        }
    }
    
    /// Set cursor position (None to hide)
    pub fn set_cursor(&mut self, position: Option<(u16, u16)>) {
        self.cursor_position = position;
    }
    
    /// Show/hide cursor
    pub fn set_cursor_visible(&mut self, visible: bool) {
        self.cursor_visible = visible;
    }
}
```

## Integration Points

### With Model::view()
```rust
trait Model {
    fn view(&self, frame: &mut Frame);
}

impl Model for MyApp {
    fn view(&self, frame: &mut Frame) {
        // Draw widgets to frame.buffer
        // Register hit regions with frame.hit_grid
        // Set cursor position if needed
        self.status_bar.render(frame);
        frame.set_cursor(Some((self.cursor_x, self.cursor_y)));
    }
}
```

### With Presenter
```rust
// Runtime uses Frame for diff and presentation
let diff = BufferDiff::compute(&prev_buffer, &frame.buffer);
presenter.present(&frame.buffer, &diff, &pool, &links)?;

// Handle cursor separately
if frame.cursor_visible {
    if let Some((x, y)) = frame.cursor_position {
        presenter.position_cursor(x, y)?;
    }
    presenter.show_cursor()?;
} else {
    presenter.hide_cursor()?;
}
```

## HitGrid (Optional Feature)
Hit grid enables mouse interaction by mapping screen positions to widget IDs.

```rust
/// Hit testing grid for mouse interaction
pub struct HitGrid {
    width: u16,
    height: u16,
    cells: Vec<Option<HitId>>,
}

/// Identifier for a clickable region
#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
pub struct HitId(pub u32);

impl HitGrid {
    pub fn register(&mut self, rect: Rect, id: HitId) {
        for y in rect.y..(rect.y + rect.height) {
            for x in rect.x..(rect.x + rect.width) {
                if let Some(cell) = self.get_mut(x, y) {
                    *cell = Some(id);
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
    pub fn hit_test(&self, x: u16, y: u16) -> Option<HitId> {
        self.get(x, y).and_then(|c| *c)
    }
}
```

# Run demo in headless mode with timeout

timeout 10s cargo run -p ftui-demo-showcase --release -- \
    --screen visual_effects \
    --headless \
    --ticks 100 \
    2>&1 | tee /tmp/text_effects_e2e.log

# Check for panics

    echo 'FAIL: Panic detected!'

# Check render times
avg_ms=$(grep 'avg_render_ms' /tmp/text_effects_e2e.log | tail -1 | cut -d= -f2)
if (( $(echo "$avg_ms > 16" | bc -l) )); then

 {"id":"bd-7nm3","title":"Virtualized List: Height Prediction (Bayes + Conformal Bounds)","description":"## Goal
Predict unseen row heights to avoid scroll jumps and reflow hitches, with formal uncertainty bounds.

## Core Idea (Alien Artifact)
Use a Bayesian online model for row height given metadata (item type, text length, wrapping). Maintain posterior for mean/variance and use conformal prediction to bound actual height:
- Posterior: h_i ~ Normal(mu_i, sigma_i^2) with conjugate updates.
- Conformal interval: [q_alpha_lower, q_alpha_upper] from calibration residuals.

Use bounds to pre-allocate scroll space; adjust only when actual height exits interval.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Scroll position remains stable; rare adjustments when bounds violated.
- Formal coverage: empirical violation rate <= alpha + tolerance.
- Deterministic under fixed seed and recorded data.

## Tests (REQUIRED)
- unit_posterior_update: mu/sigma update with new measurement.\n- unit_conformal_bounds: interval from calibration set matches alpha.\n- property_coverage: empirical violation rate <= alpha + tol.\n- e2e_scroll_stability: scripted scroll with delayed height measurements; log corrections.

## Perf

 {"id":"bd-7u8","title":"Implement cursor save/restore strategy (DEC vs ANSI vs emulated)","description":"# Implement Cursor Save/Restore Strategy

## Background
Plan Section 9.3 describes a critical cursor save/restore strategy for inline mode robustness. Terminals differ in which save/restore sequences they support reliably.

## Problem Statement
Inline mode requires saving cursor position before drawing UI and restoring after. However:
- DEC save/restore (\`ESC 7\` / \`ESC 8\`) is preferred but not universal
- ANSI save/restore (\`CSI s\` / \`CSI u\`) is an alternative
- Some terminals/muxes have quirks with either
- Must handle failures gracefully

## Layered Strategy (from Plan)

### Layer 1: DEC Save/Restore (Preferred)
\`\`\`rust
const DEC_SAVE: &[u8] = b"\x1b7";    // ESC 7 (DECSC)
const DEC_RESTORE: &[u8] = b"\x1b8"; // ESC 8 (DECRC)
\`\`\`
- Most widely supported on modern terminals
- Saves more state (cursor position, attributes, charset)
- Works in tmux/screen with passthrough

### Layer 2: ANSI Save/Restore (Fallback)
\`\`\`rust
const ANSI_SAVE: &[u8] = b"\x1b[s";    // CSI s
const ANSI_RESTORE: &[u8] = b"\x1b[u"; // CSI u
\`\`\`
- Less widely supported
- Only saves cursor position (not attributes)
- May conflict with some terminal modes

### Layer 3: Emulated (Last Resort)
\`\`\`rust
// Track cursor position explicitly
struct CursorTracker {
    x: u16,
    y: u16,
}

impl CursorTracker {
    fn save(&mut self, current_x: u16, current_y: u16) {
        self.x = current_x;
        self.y = current_y;
    }
    
    fn restore(&self, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
        write!(writer, "\x1b[{};{}H", self.y + 1, self.x + 1)
    }
}
\`\`\`
- Works everywhere that supports CUP
- Requires tracking cursor position throughout
- More overhead but guaranteed to work

## Strategy Selection
\`\`\`rust
pub enum CursorSaveStrategy {
    Dec,       // ESC 7 / ESC 8
    Ansi,      // CSI s / CSI u
    Emulated,  // Track and CUP
    Auto,      // Detect best for environment
}

impl CursorSaveStrategy {
    pub fn detect(caps: &TerminalCapabilities) -> Self {
        // Most terminals support DEC
        if !caps.in_screen {
            return Self::Dec;
        }
        // Screen has quirks, try ANSI
        Self::Ansi
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Implementation
\`\`\`rust
pub struct CursorManager {
    strategy: CursorSaveStrategy,
    saved_position: Option<(u16, u16)>,  // For emulated mode
}

impl CursorManager {
    pub fn save(&mut self, writer: &mut impl Write, current_pos: (u16, u16)) -> io::Result<()> {
        match self.strategy {
            CursorSaveStrategy::Dec => writer.write_all(DEC_SAVE),
            CursorSaveStrategy::Ansi => writer.write_all(ANSI_SAVE),
            CursorSaveStrategy::Emulated => {
                self.saved_position = Some(current_pos);
                Ok(())
            }
            CursorSaveStrategy::Auto => {
                // Try DEC, it's most common
                writer.write_all(DEC_SAVE)
            }
        }
    }
    
    pub fn restore(&mut self, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
        match self.strategy {
            CursorSaveStrategy::Dec => writer.write_all(DEC_RESTORE),
            CursorSaveStrategy::Ansi => writer.write_all(ANSI_RESTORE),
            CursorSaveStrategy::Emulated => {
                if let Some((x, y)) = self.saved_position {
                    write!(writer, "\x1b[{};{}H", y + 1, x + 1)?;
                }
                Ok(())
            }
            CursorSaveStrategy::Auto => writer.write_all(DEC_RESTORE),
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## PTY Test: Cursor Restoration
Test that verifies cursor is correctly restored:
1. Move cursor to known position
2. Save cursor
3. Move cursor elsewhere
4. Restore cursor
5. Query position (DSR) and verify matches saved position

## Tests Required
- [ ] DEC save/restore works on xterm-like terminals
- [ ] ANSI save/restore works as fallback
- [ ] Emulated mode tracks and restores correctly
- [ ] Auto-detection chooses appropriate strategy
- [ ] PTY test verifies cursor contract per strategy

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CursorManager implemented with all three strategies
- [ ] Auto-detection based on TerminalCapabilities
- [ ] Integrated into InlinePresenter
- [ ] PTY tests pass for all strategies
- [ ] Documented which terminals use which strategy
